sum: SpaceX filed a property-tax-abatement application with Grimes County on May 6, 2026 for a vertically integrated semiconductor and advanced-computing facility at the former Gibbons Creek site, ~90 miles northeast of Austin. Phase-1 investment is estimated at $55B, with total potential investment up to $119B across phases. Intel joined in April 2026.
cre: Greenfield mega-campus on a large former industrial site in rural Grimes County — in WG's home state. If approved, one of the largest single industrial commitments in U.S. history, anchoring a new supplier corridor between Bryan-College Station and Houston.

site: Advanced Technology Fabrication (prototype fab) — Gigafactory Texas
sum: Tesla is building a ~$3B prototype semiconductor fab on its existing Gigafactory Texas campus in Austin, with active construction confirmed in May 2026. The pilot line tests chip-manufacturing ideas ahead of the full-scale Terafab; small-batch production of Tesla's AI5 chip is targeted for 2026.
cre: An in-campus fab addition within Tesla's existing Austin footprint — in WG's home market. Drives demand for specialty construction, ultra-pure utilities, and process-chemical supply, plus ancillary supplier space near the campus.

site: Sherman Mega-Site — SM1 fab (first of four 300mm wafer fabs)
sum: Texas Instruments began production at SM1, the first of four planned 300mm wafer fabs on its Sherman mega-site, on December 17, 2025 — three and a half years after breaking ground. The site is planned for up to four connected fabs with a combined $40B investment and up to 3,000 direct jobs. SM2's exterior shell is already complete.
cre: Anchor industrial occupancy confirmed; ~$40B multi-phase fab campus creates sustained demand for large-bay industrial, supplier, and workforce-housing product in Grayson County and the Texoma corridor.
changeNote: Production start confirmed Dec 17, 2025 (SM1 online); SM2 shell complete; previously only announced/under construction.

site: Samsung Austin Semiconductor — Taylor Foundry Campus
sum: Samsung held an equipment move-in ceremony at the Taylor fab on April 24, 2026, marking the start of wafer-fab-equipment installation for 2nm (SF2/SF2P) production. Samsung Foundry VP Margaret Han confirmed at the SAFE Forum on May 28, 2026 that customers will begin production in 2027. Full-scale mass production has slipped from end-2026 to 2027; trial/risk production is still targeted by year-end 2026. ENF Technology (Kyle, TX) began shipping process chemicals to the fab in April 2026 — the first Korean materials supplier to deliver from U.S. soil. CHIPS Act award is $4.745 billion (finalized December 2024); total investment scaled to $37 billion.
cre: Equipment move-in and first domestic chemical supply confirm the Taylor campus is transitioning from construction to active production ramp. With pilot production by year-end 2026 and full customer runs in 2027, the fab will soon require expanded on-site chemical storage, logistics facilities, and supplier manufacturing space. The 1,235-acre campus remains the largest foundry site in the Western Hemisphere and represents significant ongoing industrial real estate demand in Williamson County.
changeNote: Multiple changes since last known snapshot (2026-02): equipment move-in ceremony held April 24, 2026; Samsung VP publicly confirmed 2027 production start at SAFE Forum May 28, 2026; full-scale mass production slipped from end-2026 to 2027 (delayed = true); first domestic chemical supply (ENF Technology, Kyle TX) activated April 2026; total investment rescaled to $37B with CHIPS award finalized at $4.745B.

site: Bastrop Advanced Chip Packaging & PCB Facility
sum: SpaceX began installing semiconductor packaging equipment at its Bastrop facility in April 2026, targeting RF chip production for Starlink by end of 2026. The expansion adds approximately 1 million sq ft to an existing ~1.1M sq ft footprint, potentially reaching 1.6M sq ft total. A $17.3M Texas Semiconductor Innovation Fund grant supports a dedicated semiconductor research and advanced packaging wing expected to create 400+ jobs. State officials describe it as the largest printed-circuit-board and panel-level packaging facility planned in North America.
cre: 1.6M-sq-ft industrial campus expanding in Bastrop County (~30 miles east of Austin) signals major occupancy and construction activity; associated Musk-entity land control (~700 acres in Bastrop County) and supplier pull create industrial and commercial leasing opportunity east of Austin.

site: John Palmour Manufacturing Center for Silicon Carbide
sum: Wolfspeed emerged from Chapter 11 bankruptcy on September 29, 2025 after cutting debt by ~70%, and the John Palmour Manufacturing Center in Siler City is now reported as operational. The company is post-restructuring and continuing to ramp its 2-million-sq-ft SiC wafer facility. The $750M CHIPS PMT had not been converted to a final award as of the bankruptcy filing, and its status post-restructuring remains unconfirmed in public sources.
cre: 2-million-sq-ft silicon-carbide wafer manufacturing plant on a 445-acre industrial campus in Chatham County is now in operational ramp phase post-bankruptcy restructuring. Ongoing ramp activity sustains demand for industrial services, utilities, and supplier facilities in the Research Triangle/Triad corridor — a market adjacent to Welcome Group's NC footprint.
changeNote: Status update to known project (2026-04 entry): Wolfspeed completed Chapter 11 reorganization and emerged from bankruptcy September 29, 2025; the Siler City John Palmour facility is now reported as operational. Debt reduced ~70%. CHIPS award final status post-restructuring not yet confirmed in sources reviewed.

site: Terafab — Grimes County site (Gibbons Creek), 22,000-acre reinvestment zone approved
sum: Grimes County commissioners voted 4-1 on June 3, 2026 to approve a 100% property-tax abatement and reinvestment zone covering more than 22,000 acres at Gibbons Creek Reservoir for SpaceX's Terafab chip-manufacturing campus. Public filings disclose Phase 1 capex of at least $55 billion, scaling to $119 billion at full buildout. Written agreements require SpaceX to invest at least $5 billion by 2030 and create 1,800 jobs by 2035; SpaceX must self-supply power and water off-grid. Agreements are partially executed — signed by the county judge but not yet countersigned by SpaceX as of June 5.
cre: Tax abatement and reinvestment zone cleared: next milestones are SpaceX countersignature, state-level permitting, and groundbreaking. A campus of this scale will require millions of square feet of heavy-industrial cleanroom space, ultra-high-purity utility infrastructure, and substantial ancillary industrial/logistics acreage — representing a generational land-and-building opportunity in Grimes County and the broader Bryan-College Station corridor.
changeNote: Major update to known project: Grimes County commissioners approved 100% tax abatement and 22,000-acre reinvestment zone on June 3, 2026. Phase 1 capex publicly disclosed at $55B (full buildout $119B). Written agreement requires $5B invested by 2030 and 1,800 jobs by 2035. Agreements partially executed as of June 5, 2026 — SpaceX signature still pending.

site: Soulbrain U.S. Manufacturing Facility & U.S. Headquarters
sum: On June 4, 2026, Texas Governor Abbott announced a $11.6 million Texas Semiconductor Innovation Fund grant to Soulbrain RASA TX LLC for its first U.S. production facility and U.S. headquarters in Taylor, TX. The facility will manufacture high-purity phosphoric acid (28,800 metric tons/year Phase 1 capacity) and other specialty wet chemicals used in wafer etching and cleaning. Phase 1 capex is $175 million (minimum $120M per grant terms); a second phase could bring the total to approximately $575 million. The project will create at least 20 high-skilled manufacturing jobs. Soulbrain is a Samsung Electronics chemical materials supplier in South Korea seeking to establish a domestic supply relationship with the Taylor foundry.
cre: New industrial chemistry plant siting in Taylor's semiconductor supplier cluster, adjacent to Samsung's 1,235-acre foundry campus. The facility requires a purpose-built chemical manufacturing and storage building with hazmat-rated design, loading infrastructure, and utility connections — a direct industrial real estate opportunity in Williamson County's fast-growing Samsung supply-chain corridor.

sum: As of January 2026, the $600 million Absolics glass substrate facility in Covington, Georgia — backed by $75 million in CHIPS Act funding — reached commercial volume sample shipments to AI chip customers including AMD. The facility is the world's first dedicated merchant plant for glass-core semiconductor substrates and is now in active production ramp with 12,000 sq-meter annual capacity. A Phase 2 expansion (targeting 72,000 sq meters) is under SKC management review; demand caution and customer qualification timelines (AMD approval near; AWS test delayed indefinitely) are factors. The 120,000-sq-ft CHIPS-funded building is constructed and operating.
cre: Operational glass-substrate plant in Newton County anchors Georgia's advanced packaging supply chain. Phase 2 capacity expansion decision — potentially adding significant additional cleanroom and utility building footprint — is pending SKC board review. Even at current Phase 1 scale, the facility generates ongoing demand for specialty chemical storage, logistics space, and supplier facilities in the Covington industrial market.

site: Linde Air Separation Plant — semiconductor-grade gases
sum: In April 2026, Linde announced plans to build an air separation facility in Garysburg, North Carolina, to produce oxygen, nitrogen, and argon for semiconductor manufacturing, healthcare, and industrial end-users. The investment exceeds $75 million and is expected to create 20 direct jobs. The plant targets the growing semiconductor manufacturing market in the region.
cre: New industrial gas plant siting in Northampton County. Air separation units require a purpose-built, heavy-utility industrial building with cryogenic storage and pipeline tie-ins — a niche but high-value industrial real estate use. Proximity to North Carolina's expanding semiconductor cluster (Wolfspeed Siler City, Research Triangle) makes this a supply-chain anchor for the state's chip-manufacturing ecosystem.

site: Samsung Austin Semiconductor — Taylor Foundry Campus, Fab 1 equipment move-in complete; Fab 2 early regulatory review initiated
sum: Samsung completed equipment move-in at its first 300mm logic fab in Taylor in April 2026 and has immediately begun early-stage regulatory review for a second fab on the same campus, targeting 2nm production. The 1,268-acre campus is now transitioning from construction to operational ramp. A second large fab building is entering pre-permit review.
cre: ~1,268-acre master-planned industrial campus now advancing from shell-complete to equipment-qualified status on Fab 1; Fab 2 pre-permit review creates near-term demand for additional heavy-industrial land, utility infrastructure, and specialized cleanroom construction subcontractors in Williamson County.
changeNote: Milestone update to known project (2026-04 entry): Fab 1 equipment move-in ceremony completed April 2026; Fab 2 entered early regulatory review April 30, 2026 — neither event was captured in the prior known-list entry.

site: Terafab — Grimes County site (Gibbons Creek), county filing documents $55B initial phase / $119B total potential capex
sum: A Grimes County filing published May 6, 2026 quantifies SpaceX's Terafab Grimes County project at $55B in initial-phase investment with a stated total potential of $119B, described as a multi-phase vertically integrated semiconductor manufacturing and advanced computing fabrication facility. This is the first published dollar figure specific to the Grimes County site, materially larger than the $25B figure associated with the Austin/Terafab announcement.
cre: Proposed multi-phase heavy-industrial campus on a 22,000-acre reinvestment zone in rural Grimes County; if phased construction proceeds, demand for large-format industrial land, logistics infrastructure, contractor laydown yards, and workforce housing in the Bryan-College Station metro corridor will be substantial. Welcome Group's Texas franchise covers this region.
changeNote: Capex update to known project (Terafab Grimes County, 2026-06 entry): county filing dated May 6, 2026 documents $55B initial / $119B total capex range for the Grimes County site specifically — prior known-list entries cited only the $25B Terafab aggregate figure.

site: Coherent InP Photonics Manufacturing Facility — Sherman Campus Expansion (6-inch Indium Phosphide Wafer Fab)
sum: Coherent broke ground June 16, 2026 on an expansion of its existing indium phosphide wafer fabrication facility in Sherman, TX. The project will double the manufacturing floor area and quadruple wafer production capacity, creating over 1,000 jobs (550+ direct). A CHIPS Act letter of intent for up to $50 million was signed with the U.S. Department of Commerce the same day; roughly $20 million in prior Texas Semiconductor Innovation Fund and Sherman EDC support also committed. NVIDIA participated in the groundbreaking ceremony and is a key customer for the optical networking components produced here.
cre: Expansion of an existing industrial manufacturing campus in Sherman (Grayson County), TX — doubling cleanroom and fab production floor area. Drives demand for industrial outdoor storage, chemical-supply logistics yards, and workforce housing near the site. Sherman already hosts Texas Instruments' SM1 fab mega-site, making the corridor an established semiconductor industrial cluster with strong co-tenant leasing potential.

site: Samsung Austin Semiconductor — Taylor Foundry Campus, mass production timeline confirmed slipped to 2027; CHIPS award finalized at $4.745B on $37B total investment
sum: Samsung's VP of North American Foundry operations publicly confirmed at Samsung's SAFE Forum USA (May 28, 2026) and per Aju News/TrendForce (June 2, 2026) that full production at the Taylor 2nm fab will begin in 2027, not end-2026 as previously stated. The CHIPS Act award has been finalized at $4.745B (reduced from $6.4B) as total planned investment scaled from $44B to $37B. Trial operations are underway; mass-production readiness is now targeted for Q4 2026 with actual volume production in early 2027. Tesla holds a ~$16.5B capacity agreement for AI6 chips. Samsung's U.S. headquarters is also reportedly relocating to Texas in 2026.
cre: Large-format advanced manufacturing campus in Taylor (Williamson County), TX — approximately 1,200+ acres. Production delay to 2027 extends the active construction and equipment-installation phase, sustaining near-term demand for contractor laydown yards, oversized-load staging areas, and industrial outdoor storage. Longer ramp timeline also defers the full workforce influx, moderating near-term multifamily pressure but extending the development window for Welcome Group build-to-suit opportunities in the Taylor/Georgetown corridor.
changeNote: Updates known project 'Samsung Electronics | Samsung Austin Semiconductor — Taylor Foundry Campus | Taylor, TX'. Key changes: (1) Mass production start formally confirmed as 2027 by Samsung VP at SAFE Forum USA May 28 2026 — a real timeline slip from the prior 'end-of-2026' target; (2) CHIPS Act award finalized at $4.745B (down from $6.4B proposed); (3) total capex investment scaled to $37B (down from $44B); (4) Samsung U.S. HQ relocation to Texas reported.

site: John Palmour Manufacturing Center for Silicon Carbide — production readiness achieved; initial 200mm SiC crystal boule growth underway; first wafer shipments to Mohawk Valley commenced
sum: Wolfspeed's John Palmour Manufacturing Center (The JP) in Siler City, NC achieved production readiness in late 2025 and is now actively growing its first batches of 200mm silicon carbide crystal boules. Initial wafer shipments to the Mohawk Valley Fab in New York have begun. The facility emerged from Wolfspeed's Chapter 11 bankruptcy (filed and resolved in 2025) with a restructured balance sheet, and is now operating on a self-funded, disciplined ramp plan tied to market recovery in SiC demand. The $750M CHIPS Act grant (contingent on refinancing) was not received prior to bankruptcy; CHIPS funding status post-restructuring is unconfirmed in current sources. The 445-acre, 2-million-sq-ft facility remains the world's largest planned 200mm SiC materials plant.
cre: Two-million-square-foot industrial manufacturing facility on a 445-acre campus in Chatham County (Siler City), NC — now operational at initial production scale. Ramp-up is ongoing and disciplined, sustaining demand for chemical/gas supply logistics, specialty industrial outdoor storage, and incremental workforce housing. The post-bankruptcy operating posture constrains near-term expansion capex but confirms the asset is producing — a positive signal for Welcome Group's broader NC industrial land thesis near the Research Triangle.
changeNote: Updates known project 'Wolfspeed | John Palmour Manufacturing Center for Silicon Carbide | Siler City, NC'. Key changes: facility has now achieved production readiness (late 2025) and is actively growing first 200mm SiC crystal boules with initial wafer shipments to Mohawk Valley underway — a confirmed production-start milestone. Company emerged from Chapter 11 bankruptcy in September 2025 with a restructured balance sheet and self-funded ramp plan.
